comment.body:
Listen, for this Powell case..
Powell will most likely wait with the rate cuts and his 'wait-and-see' stance would cause persistent uncertainity. And if the July numbers are not good, Spet cuts will be outpriced, and my read will come true.
this risks bearmarket for sure.

if Trump wants growth for any price... and thats the case, he needs a cut, for sure and dovish tone.
Simply because the admin is concerned about the recession. Thats the real risk, I been talking about it for a while it is visible in the metrics. Bonds etc. 
Trump probably was told that he should let the things unfold now, as @Joe asked, but populism and his one way eyes don't necessary let him do it + the economic warfare with China. Trump is a horrible diplomat. But not a bad man. He is hectic and impatient. And its okay at some point, but he doesn't really give room for others to decide for him.
Thats the problem.

So, it would be technically logical to force Powell to resign. But they need to have something serious against him in order to do that.
Anything.
And if a new chairman would come, and cut rates, that would be disastrous for the economy, and directly lead to a crisis later.

comment.body:
However!!
And listen...
if Trump is okay to let the things unfold earlier than later, it is good to keep Powell where he is, bcs he can later blame the recession on Powell, and the "aid" that turns the economy and market back will be his personal victory again.
So they need to play their own roles in this theater for the ppl out there (who don't understand how the economy works) to make them believe, that recession is Powell's fault, while growth is Trump's victory.
While in the first case if they would resign Powell and set a new chairman who cut rates causing crisis later, that would be Trump's direct fault... a very big one.

So place your odds... ;))

comment.body:
I don’t see how Trump could force Powell to resign. Plus, he already tried back in 2018–2019 and failed precisely because of the Fed’s independence. Unless Trump can present extremely serious cases like corruption or other major misconduct, I don’t see how he could force Powell’s removal. I mean, what political leverage could he possibly use to get him dismissed?
So it would make more sense for him to blame Powell at the end of his term for what’s about to happen, and then present himself as the one who will ‘save’ the situation

comment.body:
I had misunderstood. I thought the most logical scenario was that Trump would leave Powell in place.
But from what I’m reading now, it actually seems more likely that they’ll replace him.
Isn’t that madness though? Facing a future overheating crisis with a central bank that has no ammo left?
Is Trump really surrounded only by yes-men? Is there no one warning him about the long-term scenario risks based on actual data, which are pretty obvious, especially to America’s enemies?
I also find Alessio’s term “Amerizuela” kind of funny, but honestly, I thought debt monetization by the Fed would be the last resort.
A plan B, not plan A. Something to fall back on if rolling over long-term debt became impossible due to the lack of foreign buyers.
But now it looks like they’re just shooting themselves in the foot, willingly risking the loss of reserve currency status. Or am I wrong?
I mean if they cut now they are artificially forcing more de-dollarization.

comment.body:
Tell me about "periscope '' :  >>  "" Bottom Line
The granularity of this data set is impressive on paper: real-time, trade-level, with verified buy/sell/open/close for SPX options. However, the lack of transparency about its source, exclusivity to SPX, and absence of public audit or licensing details do raise legitimate questions about its provenance and reliability.
If you’re a trader, it’s wise to treat any “too good to be true” data set with healthy skepticism—especially when the vendor doesn’t explain how they’re able to offer something no one else can""

@unusual_whales  claim it’s not making assumptions, but it’s real simple either. It’s from CBOE or then making assumptions.
comment.raw_body:

comment.body: @Doc Trader McGraw Thanks for weighing in and I always viewed UW as being less accurate though I am not sure if OD does vastly better. For example this is the morning snapshot for Friday 6:30am from Periscope and OD. Periscope does show the large neg cluster from 6220 to 6240 which OD also shows and then negative gamma at 6270. I believe intraday updates is where OD justifies its higher value via more accuracy and periscope might be lagging. To be expected from a service charging 60$ per month vs one charging 249$ per month. I guess any options flow analyzer will have holes and it really depends on the expertise of the user to even figure out what is wrong. Most consumers might not have that level of knowledge. The expert ones should obviously pay the higher price and utilize OD.
